learning_outcomes.description: Explain and apply the technical and analytical terms used in relation to film and television
Use the language of film and television criticism and analysis in effective written and verbal communication.
Critically analyse films and television programs
Demonstrate an understanding of the industrial and institutional contexts of film and television production and reception.
Apply the skills of film and television analysis to a variety of research problems and critical approaches in screen studies and adjacent disciplines.
Build upon your introduction to and further develop your familiarity with films and television programs of different nations, cultures, and languages.

assessments.due_date:
Week 2 - Week 8

Week 10 - Week 13

Due in your tutorial each week.
assessments.task_description:
Each week in your assigned tutorial, starting in week 2, there will be a timed writing exercise that must be completed on the provided sheet. You are responsible for bringing your own pen or pencil. Responses must be handwritten unless there is an accessibility requirement. You are not permitted to access electronic devices of any kind during the writing exercise. You may consult printed or handwritten notes. You must sign the attendance sheet in your tutorial within the first thirty minutes of your tutorial commencing in order to complete the In-Tutorial Exercise for that week.

There are 10 total exercises, each is worth 3 marks. You will receive 3 marks for each complete response, 1.5 marks for a partial answer, and zero marks for responses that are incorrect or incomplete (whether unsubmitted because of non-attendance, submitted but blank, submitted but not responding to the prompt, illegible, etc.). If you have not followed an instruction to put away your device, you will be referred to an Integrity Officer for investigation.

Content Tasks (Week 2; 4-8):

For 6 weeks, you will be given a specific prompt for each exercise that will refer to content from that week’s topic. The question may be related to a still image or brief clip presented in tutorial. Feedback will be given informally via peer groups in the tutorial immediately after writing. A running total of your marks will be released part-way through semester, and your final marks will be released on blackboard during or shortly after the final teaching week. Please do not request marks from your tutor or course coordinator on an ad hoc basis.

Process Notes (Week 10-13)

In the final 4 tutorials, you will complete a series of writing exercises related to the final essay. These exercises are designed to help you conceptualise, plan, and develop your essay.

Submission of 2 out of 4 of the Process Notes tasks is a hurdle requirement to pass the Final Essay assessment. This means that if you complete fewer than 2 of the process notes exercises in class you will not be eligible to pass the Final Essay, regardless of the quality of your submission. Students who do not meet the hurdle requirement will receive a maximum of 49% for the assessment item.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: Late submission is not possible for this piece of assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.assessment_title: Audio Commentary (Sample Annotations; Recorded Commentary + Transcript)
assessments.task_description:
You are required to produce an audio commentary over one of the Commentary Clips listed in the Assessment Booklet (also posted on Blackboard). Each of the clips comes from the opening sequence of a Set Screening. It is strongly recommended that you watch the entire film that contains the clip you will be using. You are required to offer a structured, analytical commentary that explains how and why formal and/or stylistic choices shape the sequence to create meaning, deliver some sort of effect, or guide the viewer’s attention; you must make some point about why the scene you are analysing is important in the way it sets up the rest of the film. You are to download your chosen clip from Blackboard, record your audio commentary and add it to the clip. You will submit this separately from the transcript – there will be individual links on Blackboard for each component of your submission. Please see the Audio Commentary Resources section in the Assessment Booklet to help navigate the technological requirements of this piece of assessment.

There are two (2) parts to this assessment:

Part A: Sample Annotations (submitted in-person to your tutor in week 6 tutorial) [see hurdle information]

Part B: Clip with Commentary + Written Transcript

Part A: Sample Annotations (submitted in-person to your tutor in week 6 tutorial)

In the week 6 tutorial, you will submit a hard copy of handwritten, informal, sample annotations of your chosen clip, accompanying 3-5 shots from that clip. You should not aim to provide exhaustive detail of every aspect of style and form. Instead, your notes should be indicative of your thinking to show the elements of style/form you have selected, a couple of examples of how they are working in some key moments, and what it is they achieve. An exemplar of this is available on Blackboard, as is a template.

Part A (Sample Annotations) does not contribute to the mark you will receive for this piece of assessment. Your tutor will provide general feedback in the tutorial in week 7 to the class as a whole to help you develop your final submission.

Part B: Clip with Commentary + Transcript

(Transcript: 600-800 words inclusive of all referencing and formatting requirements)

This assessment is designed to evaluate your ability to select, analyse, and explain the significance of particular stylistic and formal features in a given clip. It is not a play-by-play of plot, nor is it a comprehensive account of every aspect of style and form present in the clip. You are also not pretending to be the director (and so not inventing production stories and the like). Instead, you should speak as an informed analyst explaining how the film works. There will be an exemplar on Blackboard for you to watch and listen to.

Your commentary should demonstrate your understanding of film form and style (using technical terminology from Film Art across relevant aspects of mise-en-scene/cinematography/editing/sound) as well as your capacity to make a reasonable case for the plausible intended effects of artistic decisions involving film form and style. This means that you should have a clear point you are trying to convey via your analysis, a clear rationale for the features you are focusing on, and a sense of how the different features are working together.

It is important that you prioritise depth over breadth: do not try to cover every single feature, do not make general claims, do not make assertions without any kind of evidence through analysis, do not merely describe what happens on screen. It is important to identify relevant techniques, but you need to go to the next step and explain what they do and why they are important. It is also critical that you make some claim about how your chosen scene sets up the film as a whole – that you offer an informed and reasonable suggestion of the importance of the style and/or form you focus on to the way the film unfolds.

Please consult the exemplar on Blackboard. You only need to refer to Film Art as shown in the transcript exemplar on Blackboard – production trivia and scholarly sources will negatively impact your work. Focus on your own original insight and analysis of the clip.

Please Note: The film you analyse for this piece of assessment cannot be analysed in your Final Essay.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Final Essay (Process Notes; Comparative Analysis Essay)
assessments.task_description:
Your task is to write an essay wherein you make an argument via the analysis of your clips in response to your chosen question, supported by an appropriate use of scholarly material. Your analysis should take into account relevant stylistic and formal features of your chosen clips. The features that are relevant will depend on the argument you are making, and the argument itself should involve a coherent and sensible rationale for the clips that you are comparing. It is important that your essay responds clearly to your chosen question.

There are two (2) parts to this assessment:

Part A: Process Notes [see hurdle information]

Part B: Comparative Analysis Essay

Process Notes: In tutorials, you will complete a series of writing exercises related to conceptualising your essay. These are marked as part of the "weekly tutorial writing task" series, but are a hurdle requirement for the final essay -- you must submit 2/4 of these tasks in-person to pass the essay. The word count does not apply to the process notes.

Comparative Analysis Essay

Full details are provided in the Assessment Booklet.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2 Tutorial (Form: Parts and the Whole)

Please do the reading, watch the screening and view the lecture prior to coming to tutorials. Tutorials are designed to consolidate student's knowledge of film and television concepts from the readings and lecture, and build their analytical skills. Each week there is a graded tutorial writing task, followed by tutorial activities carried out individually, in groups, and as a class.
